Competitor Research: Understanding Your
Market Landscape
This document explores the critical process of competitor research for effective marketing and copywriting. Following our
previous exploration of product market awareness, we now focus on understanding what competitors in your space are
selling, how they're communicating with the market, what they're promising, and what customers like or dislike about them.
This research forms a crucial part of creating a comprehensive brief that will inform your copywriting and marketing
strategies.
The insights gathered through competitor research will help you identify effective messaging, understand market positioning,
and discover opportunities to differentiate your offering. By analyzing competitors' value propositions and customer
feedback, you'll be better equipped to create compelling copy that resonates with your target audience and addresses their
specific needs and pain points.

-- 1 of 8 --

The Purpose of Competitor Research
Competitor research serves as a critical component in developing effective marketing strategies and compelling copy. The
primary goal is to create a comprehensive document that contains all the research context needed when working with large
language models (LLMs) for generating copy.
When developing marketing materials, understanding your competition provides invaluable insights that can inform your
approach. This research helps identify:
Value Propositions
Identifying what competitors
are promising to customers and
how they position their
products in the market. These
insights reveal the claims and
benefits that resonate with your
target audience.
Customer Feedback
Understanding what consumers
like and dislike about competing
products helps identify
opportunities for differentiation
and improvement. This feedback
reveals pain points you can
address and benefits you can
emphasize.
Messaging
Frameworks
Analyzing how competitors
speak to the market provides
insights into effective
communication strategies,
including language, tone, and
specific phrases that connect
with customers.
This research, combined with product market awareness and psychographic research, creates a comprehensive foundation for
developing effective copy. The goal is to compile all this information into a document that can guide LLMs in generating
targeted, compelling marketing content.

-- 2 of 8 --

The Competitor Research Process
The competitor research process involves a systematic approach to gathering and analyzing information about competitors in
your market space. This process helps identify key insights that can inform your marketing strategy and copywriting efforts.
Define Your Research Parameters
Clearly specify the product you're researching, the
geographic market you're focusing on, and the type of
competitors you're interested in (e.g., DTC brands, e-
commerce businesses, direct response marketers).
Identify Key Competitors
Determine which companies or brands are directly
competing with your product or service. Focus on those
with similar value propositions or those targeting the
same customer base.
Analyze Competitor Messaging
Examine their core messaging, hooks, angles, and big
ideas that appear consistently in their advertising assets.
Look for patterns in how they communicate benefits and
address pain points.
Evaluate Customer Feedback
Research what customers like and dislike about
competing products through reviews, social media, and
other platforms. This reveals opportunities for
differentiation and improvement.
When conducting this research, it's important to be flexible and willing to experiment with different approaches. You may
need to modify your research parameters or use multiple AI tools to get the most comprehensive insights. The goal is to gather
information that will be most relevant and useful for your specific marketing needs.

-- 3 of 8 --

Using AI Tools for Competitor Research
AI-powered research tools can significantly streamline the competitor research process, providing comprehensive insights in a
fraction of the time it would take to gather manually. Different AI models may excel at different aspects of research, making it
valuable to use multiple tools for a complete picture.
Benefits of AI-Powered Research
Rapidly processes vast amounts of information from
multiple sources
Identifies patterns and trends across competitor
messaging
Summarizes key findings in structured, actionable
formats
Provides objective analysis of market positioning
Saves significant time compared to manual research
methods
Recommended AI Research Tools
Gemini: Excellent for comprehensive overviews and
detailed competitor profiles
ChatGPT: Strong at identifying specific phrases and
claims used by competitors
Perplexity: Good at finding additional competitors and
supplementary information
Claude: Helpful for analyzing copy patterns and
messaging frameworks
When using AI tools for research, it's important to be specific in your prompts. For example, you might ask: "I want your help
doing competitor research for a product in [your niche]. The specific product is [product], and we are focused on competitors
in [commerce and geography]." Follow with specific questions about target demographics, acquisition funnels, core messaging,
pricing, and customer feedback.
If you don't get the information you need from one AI tool, try refining your prompt or switching to a different tool. Each has
different strengths and may provide unique insights that others miss. The goal is to compile a comprehensive view of your
competitive landscape.

-- 4 of 8 --

Case Study: Eye Serum Competitor
Research
Examining competitor research for an eye serum product reveals valuable insights about the skincare market, particularly for
products targeting fine lines, wrinkles, and under-eye concerns. This case study demonstrates how competitor analysis can
inform your marketing strategy.
Core Messaging Patterns
Successful eye serums consistently emphasize being
"lightweight" and "non-sticky" while promising to
"reduce puffiness," "diminish fine lines," and "improve
elasticity." Clinical claims are prominent, with specific
percentages of improvement (e.g., "97% showed
improvement in under-eye bags after 8 weeks").
Key Ingredients Focus
Leading brands highlight specific active ingredients like
retinol, peptides, caffeine, and niacinamide. The
concentration of these ingredients is often emphasized
(e.g., "0.1% vegan retinol, 3% caffeine") to establish
credibility and effectiveness.
Positive Customer Feedback
Customers consistently praise products that show rapid
results, have pleasant textures that absorb well, create a
good base for makeup, and come in hygienic packaging.
The perception of immediate effectiveness is particularly
valued.
Negative Customer Feedback
Common complaints include skin irritation (burning,
rashes, peeling), products that pill or create a film, lack of
visible results despite premium pricing, and sensitivity
issues particularly with certain ingredients like
niacinamide.
The research revealed three dominant messaging pillars in the DTC skincare market: science versus nature (emphasizing
either clinical research or natural ingredients), transparency and affordability (highlighting honest pricing and ingredient
disclosure), and community and authenticity (focusing on real results and customer experiences).
This competitive analysis provides a clear picture of what claims resonate with customers, what ingredients to highlight, what
benefits to emphasize, and what potential objections to address in marketing materials for a new eye serum product.

-- 5 of 8 --

Case Study: Airbnb Arbitrage Coaching
Programs
The competitive landscape for Airbnb arbitrage coaching programs reveals interesting patterns in how these businesses
market their services. This case study examines the messaging, acquisition strategies, and customer feedback for programs
teaching people how to lease properties long-term and manage them as short-term rentals.
Core Messaging Themes
The market is characterized by powerful narratives
centered around financial freedom without property
ownership. Key messaging includes:
"No property needed, use other people's homes"
"Quit your 9-5 with Airbnb arbitrage"
"Generate six-figure income fast"
"Zero down real estate - control properties you don't
own"
"Build a business that runs on autopilot"
Market Evolution
The research revealed an interesting market shift: more
sophisticated competitors are pivoting from pure rental
arbitrage to co-hosting or property management models.
This shift addresses several market challenges:
Lower capital requirements (no need for
$10,000-$15,000 per property)
Reduced risk (service business vs. lease-holding
business)
Response to market saturation and rising long-term
rents
More sustainable business model in maturing market
The acquisition funnel for these programs typically follows a consistent pattern: extensive free content on platforms like
YouTube builds audience and authority, which funnels into free webinars that serve as the primary sales vehicle for high-ticket
coaching programs ranging from $2,500 to over $10,000.
Customer feedback reveals a significant divide. Supporters appreciate the free content and view the coaches as authentic
entrepreneurs willing to share knowledge. Critics point to ethical concerns about advice regarding landlord disclosure,
question the actual success of the coaches' own rental properties, and express frustration with the high cost of programs
relative to the value received.
This analysis provides valuable insights for positioning a new coaching program in this space, highlighting opportunities to
address ethical concerns, demonstrate legitimate success, and provide tangible value beyond basic information.

-- 6 of 8 --

Case Study: Golf Driver Marketing
The market for golf drivers targeting amateur players reveals fascinating insights about effective messaging and positioning.
This case study examines how different brands market drivers that promise to fix slices and increase distance, with a particular
focus on direct response versus traditional marketing approaches.
Pain Point Identification
The research identified two
dominant pain points for amateur
golfers: the persistent slice (a
technical flaw causing balls to curve
dramatically right for right-handed
players) and lack of distance off the
tee. These frustrations significantly
impact enjoyment of the game.
Direct Response
Messaging
The most successful brand
(Performance Golf) uses highly
specific direct response messaging:
"The world's first slice-fixing driver
made for amateurs" and "Fix your
slice without changing your swing."
Their revenue ($100M) far exceeds
competitors using more general
messaging.
Specific Claims &
Promises
Winning phrases include "instantly
fix your slice," "go from weak slices to
hitting flush straight shots and even
baby draws just after one shot,"
"make slicing virtually impossible,"
and "add 20-30 yards of distance."
The contrast between direct response and traditional marketing approaches is striking. Brands using specific, benefit-focused
messaging that directly addresses pain points (Performance Golf) significantly outperform those using more general quality
and design messaging (Sub70, Stix) despite customer complaints about product quality.
Customer feedback reveals important insights. Positive reviews emphasize newfound confidence, hitting fairways
consistently, and renewed enjoyment of the game. Negative feedback centers on products not working as advertised, poor
build quality (clubs breaking or underperforming), and perceptions that physical products are merely "Trojan horses" to sell
digital training.
This analysis demonstrates the power of specific, pain-point focused messaging over general quality claims in the golf
equipment market, providing a clear direction for marketing a new driver with similar benefits.

-- 7 of 8 --

Key Takeaways and Implementation
Strategy
Effective competitor research provides invaluable insights that can significantly improve your marketing strategy and
copywriting. Based on the case studies and methodologies explored, here are the key takeaways and implementation
strategies for your own competitor research.
1 Focus on Core Messaging and
Customer Feedback
While comprehensive research is valuable, prioritize
understanding competitors' core messaging (the
claims and promises they make) and what customers
like and dislike about their products. These elements
provide the most actionable insights for developing
your own marketing strategy.
2 Use Multiple AI Tools for Complete
Insights
Different AI tools excel at different aspects of
research. If one tool doesn't provide the specific
information you need, try refining your prompt or
switching to another tool. Gemini may provide
excellent overviews while ChatGPT might better
identify specific phrases and claims used by
competitors.
3 Identify Specific Language and
Claims
Pay special attention to the exact phrases, sentences,
and claims competitors use in their marketing. These
provide a vocabulary of proven language that
resonates with your target audience and can be
adapted for your own marketing materials.
4 Learn from Direct Response Success
As demonstrated in the golf driver case study, specific,
benefit-focused direct response messaging often
outperforms general quality claims. Identify the most
successful competitors in your space and analyze
their approach to pain points and promises.
When implementing your research findings, compile them into a comprehensive document that can be referenced when
creating marketing materials or working with AI tools to generate copy. This document should include key competitor
messaging, customer feedback patterns, specific language and claims that resonate with the market, and opportunities for
differentiation based on competitor weaknesses.
Remember that competitor research is one component of a larger research strategy that includes product market awareness
and psychographic research. In the next module, we'll explore psychographic research to understand deeper pain points,
hopes, and dreams of your target market, which will complete your research foundation for effective copywriting.

-- 8 of 8 --
